fuzzy_prd: We need the full unidirectional state-container and stream demo surface covered by the tests: reducer-driven Kaskade state updates, flow/live/replay stream behavior, single-event buffering, clear/reset operations, normalized errors, and the sample media-player state machine. The player scenario has a fixed playlist and deterministic play/pause/next/previous/stop transitions; its output is part of the same black-box contract as the lower-level stream scenarios.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with scenario) -> stdout state/flow/player lines

trigger_keywords: what happens when subscriber attaches before any actions
does the initial state get delivered automatically
when does the listener first receive a value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When onStateChanged is set, the container immediately drains a stateQueue that has been accumulating states. The initial state is always the first element of that queue, so attaching a listener always delivers the initial state first, even if no actions have been processed.

trigger_keywords: what is the error format for unregistered action
how to signal unknown action label
error output when no reducer is registered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Processing an action with no registered reducer emits two lines: first 'error=unhandled_action', then 'action=<label>' where <label> is the exact string of the unrecognized action. Processing after teardown emits only 'error=unsubscribed'.

trigger_keywords: do single-event states update the current state
how does SingleEvent affect the state queue
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A state implementing SingleEvent is delivered to listeners but is never saved back as currentState. The container tracks the previous non-SingleEvent state and continues to use it for the next reduction. In the DamEmitter (replay stream), SingleEvent values are also excluded from savedValues and never replayed to new subscribers.

trigger_keywords: what is the dam emitter replay rule
how does the replaying stream retain values per type
which values does the DamEmitter save
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: DamEmitter stores the latest value per Kotlin KClass (one entry per concrete type). On new subscription it replays all saved values. Values that implement SingleEvent are never saved. After clear(), savedValues is empty so a new subscriber gets nothing from the past.

trigger_keywords: what is the scenario field for the state container
how to select kaskade vs flow mode
what does the flow field do in kaskade scenario
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter input uses 'scenario' to select mode: 'kaskade' runs the state container with optional 'flow' field ('dam' wraps it in a DamEmitter). 'flow' with 'kind' 'plain' runs a bare MutableEmitter; 'flow' with 'kind' 'dam' runs a DamEmitter directly. The player scenario uses 'scenario':'player'.

Full Kaskade public surface: scenario=kaskade for reducer state updates and single events, scenario=flow for live/replay/DAM/plain flow behavior, and scenario=player for the fixed playlist media-player state machine including play, pause, next, previous, stop, and emitted player state.

Route by top-level scenario. Preserve exact output lines for initial state, reducer ops, single_events, flow kind, action sequences, player playlist position/title/status, and normalized errors from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

The player scenario is pass-critical. It uses the fixed playlist and action sequence semantics from feature4_media_player: play/pause toggle playback state, next/previous move through the playlist with the PRD's boundary/wrap behavior, stop returns to the stopped state, and every emitted line must preserve title/index/status exactly as public tests require.

trigger_keywords: Kaskade buffered replay live stream single events
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Replay streams must deliver buffered state to late subscribers; live streams only deliver future updates; single events persist until consumed as specified; clear/reset removes buffered replay values according to scenario; unhandled or unsubscribed errors render normalized error lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle the ephemeral-notification convention the same way as the core container does
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The DamEmitter's saveValue() method checks 'if (value !is SingleEvent)' before storing, mirroring how the state container itself never sets currentState to a SingleEvent. Both the Kaskade container and the DamEmitter share this exclusion rule.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the pattern used in the settings module — listeners and stream wiring work the same way
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The MutableEmitter/Emitter abstraction in dev.gumil.kaskade.flow. The stateEmitter() and stateDamEmitter() extension functions wire onStateChanged to sendValue, making Kaskade the source for observable streams.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under kaskade/core/src/commonMain/kotlin/dev/gumil/kaskade/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_1_state_reduction.json.description: A state container is created with an initial state and a set of action-to-state reducers. As soon as a listener is attached it receives the initial state, and thereafter every processed action is reduced to a new state which is delivered to the listener in order. Each emitted state is reported on its own line using its opaque state label.

feature1_2_buffered_replay.json.description: States produced before any listener is attached are buffered in order, and the buffer is replayed to the first listener the moment it attaches. The buffer always begins with the initial state, followed by one state per action processed while no listener was present. After the buffered states are replayed, subsequent processing continues to be delivered live.

feature1_3_single_event.json.description: A state may be declared as a single event: it is delivered to the listener exactly like any other state, but it is never retained as the container's current state. After a single event is emitted, the next reduction still observes the previously persisted (non-event) state, so processing the same regular action again yields the same result as before the single event occurred.

feature1_4_errors.json.description: The container rejects two illegal situations with normalized error signals. Processing an action that has no registered reducer is reported as an unhandled-action error together with the offending action label. Processing any action after the container has been torn down (its bindings cleared) is reported as an unsubscribed error. Any states delivered before the error still appear, then the normalized error terminates the run.

feature2_live_stream.json.description: A live observable stream delivers values to its subscriber only while the subscription is active. Values emitted before a subscriber attaches are not buffered and are never seen. Once a subscriber detaches, further values are not delivered to it. Each delivered value is reported on its own line.

feature3_1_replay_stream.json.description: A replaying observable stream remembers the latest value it has seen for each value type and replays the remembered value(s) to every new subscriber the instant it attaches, in addition to forwarding live values. When several values share the same type only the most recent one is retained. A value sent while a subscriber is attached is delivered live; after detaching and re-attaching, the retained latest value is replayed again. While no subscriber is attached, values are still recorded but not delivered.

feature3_2_replay_clear.json.description: The replaying stream can have its remembered values cleared. After a clear, a newly attaching subscriber receives nothing from the past, confirming the retained value was truly discarded rather than merely hidden. Values delivered live before the clear still appear in the output.

feature3_3_replay_single_event.json.description: When a state container is observed through a replaying stream, the latest regular state is replayed to a new subscriber, but single-event states are never retained for replay. Reducing an action to a regular state records it for replay; emitting a single event delivers it live only; on detaching and re-attaching, only the latest regular state is replayed while the single event is omitted.

feature4_media_player.json.description: A media-player state machine drives a fixed circular playlist of tracks through actions. The playlist, in order, is: Like Ooh-Ahh, Cheer up, TT, Knock Knock, Signal, One More Time, Likey, Heart Shaker, Candy Pop, What Is Love?, Wake Me Up, Dance The Night Away, BDZ, Yes Or Yes, The Best Thing I Ever Did. The machine starts stopped. A pause_play toggles between playing and paused: from a stopped or paused position it begins playing the current track (the first track when nothing has played yet); while already playing it pauses. A next moves forward one track (and starts playing), a previous moves back one track (and starts playing); both wrap around the ends of the playlist. A stop halts playback and resets the position so the next play starts from the first track. Each delivered state is reported on its own line: a playing state as the played track preceded by a label, a paused state, or a stopped state. Only states produced by processed actions are reported (the implicit initial stopped state is not).
